ure_rules.criteria: severity = High AND (age > 50 OR occupation_risk = High)
ure_rules.description: Conservative blanket exclusion for high-severity risk clusters; ignores treatment adherence.

ure_rules.criteria: condition = diabetes AND severity IN (Medium, High)
ure_rules.description: Applies fixed loading for diabetes medium/high severity without HbA1c trend nuance.

ure_rules.criteria: condition = mental health AND severity = High
ure_rules.description: Strict exclusion; does not differentiate controlled cases with active treatment.

ure_rules.criteria: condition = headache AND severity = Medium AND smoking = Yes
ure_rules.description: Escalates medium headaches in smokers to referral, even if no neurological red flags.
Routes to referral regardless of claim type or time elapsed.
Simplistic acceptance for young applicants; misses occupational hazards.
Generic low-severity acceptance rule with minimal context awareness.
Fallback rule used when no specific rule applies; intentionally broad and rigid.
Legacy heuristic rule retained for backward compatibility; edge-case coverage is incomplete.
Legacy heuristic rule retained for backward compatibility; edge-case coverage is incomplete.
Legacy heuristic rule retained for backward compatibility; edge-case coverage is incomplete.
Overly strict rule lacking context; can reject manageable moderate-risk cases.

divergence_scenarios.detailed_explanation: No explicit URE rule captured combined pattern (headache, severity Medium, occupation risk High, income variability Unstable); human underwriter considered broader context while URE fell back to generic decision logic.

URE rule treated case as lower risk due to simplified criteria, but human reviewer elevated risk after considering occupation (Driver) and medical history signals ['anxiety episodes', 'tobacco exposure'].

URE rule treated case as lower risk due to simplified criteria, but human reviewer elevated risk after considering occupation (Construction Worker) and medical history signals ['anxiety episodes', 'family history of chronic illness'].

No explicit URE rule captured combined pattern (headache, severity Low, occupation risk Low, income variability Stable); human underwriter considered broader context while URE fell back to generic decision logic.

No explicit URE rule captured combined pattern (headache, severity Low, occupation risk High, income variability Stable); human underwriter considered broader context while URE fell back to generic decision logic.

URE rule treated case as lower risk due to simplified criteria, but human reviewer elevated risk after considering occupation (Self-Employed) and medical history signals ['recurrent migraine', 'family history of chronic illness'].
URE applied a hard threshold rule for headache that ignored compensating factors such as non-smoking status and manageable claims history, leading to a harsher action than human review.

No explicit URE rule captured combined pattern (mental health, severity Medium, occupation risk Medium, income variability Unstable); human underwriter considered broader context while URE fell back to generic decision logic.

URE rule treated case as lower risk due to simplified criteria, but human reviewer elevated risk after considering occupation (Driver) and medical history signals ['elevated blood pressure', 'family history of chronic illness'].
URE applied a hard threshold rule for diabetes that ignored compensating factors such as non-smoking status and manageable claims history, leading to a harsher action than human review.
URE applied a hard threshold rule for mental health that ignored compensating factors such as non-smoking status and manageable claims history, leading to a harsher action than human review.
URE applied a hard threshold rule for headache that ignored compensating factors such as non-smoking status and manageable claims history, leading to a harsher action than human review.
Rule criteria boundary was ambiguous around severity and lifestyle thresholds; URE selected Load while human selected Refer after qualitative evidence weighting.
Rule criteria boundary was ambiguous around severity and lifestyle thresholds; URE selected Accept while human selected Refer after qualitative evidence weighting.
URE applied a hard threshold rule for mental health that ignored compensating factors such as stable treatment adherence and manageable claims history, leading to a harsher action than human review.

URE rule treated case as lower risk due to simplified criteria, but human reviewer elevated risk after considering occupation (Driver) and medical history signals ['glucose variability', 'neuropathy symptoms'].
